name: Ginger Root Extract
historicalUse: Ginger is among the most universally documented medicinal plants in human history. It appears in the Charaka Samhita (Ayurvedic, ~300 BCE), the Shennong Bencao Jing (Chinese, ~200 CE), and extensively in Arabic medicine — Ibn Sina devotes considerable attention to zanjabil in the Canon, prescribing it for digestive weakness, nausea, joint pain, and as a warming agent for cold temperaments. Rambam's Treatise on Asthma specifically recommends ginger preparations for respiratory and digestive complaints and it appears in his pharmaceutical compendium. The 16th-century Ottoman physician Gevrekzade lists ginger among essential warming digestives.
modernEvidence: Ginger is one of the most extensively studied botanical agents. Over 100 clinical trials have evaluated its efficacy in nausea (chemotherapy-induced, pregnancy, motion sickness), gastric motility, inflammation, and pain. Active constituents include gingerols (fresh root), shogaols (dried/processed), and zingerone. Mechanistically, ginger acts as a 5-HT3 antagonist and NK1 receptor antagonist, explaining antiemetic effects. It also inhibits COX-1/COX-2 and 5-LOX pathways.
mechanisms: Antiemetic: 5-HT3 and NK1 receptor antagonism in GI tract and CNS
Prokinetic: accelerates gastric emptying, reduces intestinal transit time
Anti-inflammatory: COX-1/COX-2 and 5-LOX pathway inhibition via gingerols/shogaols
Thermogenic: activates TRPV1 receptors, increases metabolic heat production
Carminative: relaxes lower esophageal sphincter and intestinal smooth muscle
benefits: Supports normal gastric motility and comfortable digestion
safetyProfile: Excellent safety profile at food and therapeutic doses. GRAS status (FDA). Mild heartburn or reflux possible in sensitive individuals at high doses.
contraindications: Caution in patients with coagulation disorders at high doses (>4g/day)
Caution pre-surgery — theoretical antiplatelet activity at high doses
drugInteractions: Anticoagulants (warfarin, aspirin) — theoretical additive antiplatelet effect at high doses; monitor INR
Diabetes medications — may have additive glucose-lowering effect; monitor blood glucose
Nifedipine and cardiac medications — case reports of potentiation at high doses
physicianNotes: Among the safest and best-evidenced botanical agents. Clinically appropriate across a wide patient population. At 150mg standardized extract (5% gingerols) per serving, well within safe limits even for patients on anticoagulation if monitored. Notably useful in patients with post-operative or chemotherapy-related nausea (at higher doses).
patientGuidance: Can be taken with or without food. Mild warming sensation in the GI tract is normal and expected. If you take blood thinners, inform your physician.
